Forty-five little cherubs without
q place to care for them and pro-
tect them from the elements and
cold chills of winter, is the prob
lem which is now confronting the
Woman's club in regard to the
Children's Day Nursery.


There have been so many hard
working mothers, who have
brought their children to the nur-
sery to be cared for while they toll
day in and day out, that the pres
ent home will no longer accommo-
date the little ones and the women
of the club have been forced to
hunt new quarters.


1--WE have searched diligently for
q convenient home of seven or
eight rooms" said Mrs. c. w. De-
ming, who has been appointed to
head the committee to secure a
new building, "but our efforts have
been fruitless. We have found
satisfactory places and we have the
money to afford them, but the
